Styles › Neo-Classical

1750s – 1900s (revived since) · Europe → Global

Neo-Classical

Traditional, formal, symmetrical — columns and proportion.

  • Overview
  • Design Philosophy
  • Visual Characteristics
  • Materials
  • Color Palette
  • Façade Guide
  • Lighting
  • Building Types
  • Design Checklist
  • Common Mistakes
  • AI Prompts
  • Gallery
  • Related

01 — Overview

Overview

Definition. Neoclassical architecture revives the orders and proportions of Greece and Rome — symmetrical, column-fronted, dignified exteriors of stone expressing reason, permanence and civic authority (secular civic examples only).

Historical background. Driven by 18th-century archaeology and Enlightenment ideals, it became the language of museums, parliaments, banks and monuments worldwide.

Origin. France, Britain and Italy, mid-18th century.

Timeline

1748

Excavation of Pompeii fuels revival

1830

Greek Revival peaks; Altes Museum

1852

British Museum façade completed

1900

Beaux-Arts civic grandeur worldwide

Key Architects

Karl Friedrich SchinkelRobert SmirkeClaude-Nicolas LedouxJohn Soane

Influential Projects

British Museum

Robert Smirke · 1852
London, UK

Altes Museum

Karl Friedrich Schinkel · 1830
Berlin, Germany

Brandenburg Gate

Carl Gotthard Langhans · 1791
Berlin, Germany

02 — Design Philosophy

Design Philosophy

Core Principles

The classical ordersSymmetry & axialityHarmonic proportionHierarchy & decorumPermanence

The Thinking

Universal rational rules of beauty inherited from antiquity: proportion governs all, the orders provide a disciplined grammar, and symmetry expresses order and civic virtue.

Values It Represents

ReasonAuthorityCivic virtueTimelessness

Why It Emerged

Enlightenment rationalism and admiration for Greece and Rome — architecture to embody law, reason and enduring institutions.

03 — Visual Characteristics

Visual Characteristics

MassingSymmetrical balanced blocks with a dominant central portico and wings.
Form LanguageClear geometric volumes governed by the columned portico front.
CompositionStrict axial symmetry; base–piano nobile–entablature.
ProportionFrom the classical orders; columns set the module.
GeometryRectilinear with the pediment triangle and colonnade.
RhythmRegular colonnades — measured intercolumniation.
SymmetryStrict bilateral symmetry about a central axis.
ScaleMonumental and dignified.
Roof TypesLow pitched roofs behind parapets; pediments crowning.
Window ProportionVertically proportioned, symmetric, often with pediments/architraves.
EntranceGrand central portico with columns and pediment on a podium.
Façade ArticulationColumned orders, entablatures, pediments and pilasters in stone over a rusticated base.

04 — Materials

Materials

Limestone & marble

Noble, durable, carvable — the medium of the orders.

Ashlar stone

Precisely cut blocks expressing refinement.

Rusticated stone

Heavy grooved masonry grounding the base.

Bronze

Doors, statuary and enrichment.

05 — Color Palette

Color Palette

Dignified, serene, authoritative — warm stone neutrals with noble accents.

Primary

Limestone Cream
#e4dac6
Marble White
#efe9dc

Accent

Verdigris Bronze
#5e7d6f
Pompeian Red
#9c3b2e

Material Colors

Stone Grey
#c1b6a0
Gold Leaf
#c8a14a

Typical Combinations

  • +All-stone monochrome with bronze
  • +Cream stone + Pompeian red

06 — Façade Design Guide

Façade Design Guide

Window CompositionVertically proportioned, symmetric, in regular bays; crown with pediments/architraves.
EmphasisBalanced — vertical columns within a horizontal entablature.
Shading DevicesDeep porticoes, colonnades and loggias for shade and shadow.
BalconiesStone balustraded balconies on the principal floor.
FramesStone architraves, pediments and surrounds.
OpeningsSymmetrical and hierarchical — grander on the principal floor.
Architectural DetailsCornices, dentils, capitals, pilasters per the chosen order.
OrnamentationEssential but rule-bound — derived from the orders, correct and hierarchical.

07 — Lighting

Lighting

Natural Lighting Strategy

Colonnades choreograph light and shadow; symmetric openings give even daylight.

Artificial Lighting Concept

Warm light grazing stone; uplit columns.

Night Façade Appearance

Floodlit porticoes; columns modelled by uplight; pediment silhouetted.

Recommendations

Warm 2700K to flatter stone; uplight columns and pediment.

08 — Typical Building Types

Typical Building Types

Where this style performs best:

Museums & galleriesParliaments & courtsBanksUniversitiesMemorialsLibraries

09 — Design Checklist

Design Checklist

Tap an item to mark it complete — saved to your browser.

  • Establish strict symmetry about a central axis
  • Front with a columned portico and pediment
  • Choose and correctly proportion an order
  • Organise base–piano nobile–entablature
  • Raise on a podium with steps
  • Use stone with carved mouldings
  • Keep ornament rule-bound and hierarchical
  • Crown with pediment or balustraded parapet

10 — Common Mistakes

Common Mistakes

Elements that contradict the style — what to avoid:

  • ✕Incorrect proportions or mixed orders
  • ✕Asymmetry breaking the discipline
  • ✕Thin 'stuck-on' columns with no entablature logic
  • ✕Over- or under-scaled details
  • ✕Mirror glass clashing with the language

11 — AI Prompt Examples

AI Prompt Examples

Optimised for Midjourney, ChatGPT image tools or Claude — exterior renders. Tap copy to use.

Exterior

Neoclassical museum exterior, symmetrical limestone façade, grand colonnade and pediment, raised on a podium with steps, blue sky, monumental, frontal architectural photography --ar 3:2 --style raw

Façade Concept

Neoclassical façade elevation, regular colonnade, entablature with dentils, pedimented windows, rusticated base, strict symmetry, stone, high detail

Night View

Neoclassical civic building exterior at night, floodlit colonnade, uplit columns casting deep shadow, dignified, architectural night photography

Aerial View

Aerial of a Neoclassical civic complex, symmetrical wings around a central portico, axial forecourt, formal gardens, drone view

Street Perspective

Street view approaching a Neoclassical museum, grand portico and steps, columns, pedestrians for scale, dignified, documentary photography

Masterplan

Neoclassical civic masterplan, strict axial symmetry, ceremonial boulevard, formal squares, Beaux-Arts plan, stone palette

Related Styles

1920s – 1940s

Art Deco

Glamorous, geometric, decorative — strong lines and symmetry.

France → USA → GlobalGlamorous
1920s – 1970s

Modern

Clean lines, functional forms, no ornament.

Europe → GlobalRational
2000s – present

Contemporary

Current trends, evolving — mixed materials, clean geometry.
